text: In this video, we'll be describing laparoscopic myomectomy for a patient having a large anterior fibroid. At our institute, we either prefer to avoid vasopressin and when we are using vasopressin, we use it at only seven point five units diluted in three hundred ml of normal saline.
The surgeon injects a diluted vasopressin solution into the fibroid capsule, facilitating aqua dissection that visibly releases the fibroid from the uterine cavity and bed.

The surgeon prepares to suture the large bed left by the anterior intramural fibroid using a multilayered approach with a quill suture. The baseball suturing technique is employed to obliterate the myoma bed and achieve hemostasis, with sutures placed either ipsilaterally or contralaterally.

The surgeon uses ipsilateral baseball suturing on the myoma bed, with no visible hematoma formation. The dissection is completed efficiently to prevent bleeding before the vasopressin effect diminishes.

The surgeon completes suturing the deeper layer of the myoma bed and begins suturing the external layer using baseball sutures. The sutures overlap on the external surface, minimizing contact with the peritoneum and reducing the risk of omental adhesions.
The surgeon demonstrates the ease of the suturing technique by pulling the suture out while applying gentle traction, ensuring the tissue approximates along the suture line.

The surgeon completes the suturing of the myoma bed using a quill suture, ensuring uniform tension across the suture length for optimal tissue healing. The final view shows a bloodless myoma bed, fully sutured with no visible bleeding.
